input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-13T10:08:50.341Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference - Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated zero tests as a pass.

Let me look at their project files to understand the setup.

data.content:
Exit code **8** in Microsoft.Testing.Platform means "no tests discovered." Since `INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` isn't defined, all tests are compiled out, and the new runner treats that as an error (VSTest did not).

The fix is a single property in your `.csproj`:

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered in this assembly -->
    <TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>false</TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>

data.reasoningText:
The issue is clear. With Microsoft.Testing.Platform (enabled by `EnableMSTestRunner`), exit code 8 means "no tests were found." When `INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` is not defined, all tests are compiled out, resulting in zero tests, which causes exit code 8.

The fix is to set the MSBuild property `<TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>false</TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>` in the project file. This tells Microsoft.Testing.Platform to return exit code 0 when no tests are discovered.

arguments.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered in this assembly -->
    <TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>false</TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>

toolArgs.new_str:
<EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
    <!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered in this assembly -->
    <TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>false</TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>

     <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>

�Unknown option '--nologo'
Usage TestProject [option providers] [extension option providers]

Execute a .NET Test Application.

Options:
    --config-file
        Specifies a testconfig.json file.

    --diagnostic
        Enable the diagnostic logging. The default log level is 'Trace'.
        The file will be written in the output directory with the name log_[yyMMddHHmmssfff].diag

    --diagnostic-filelogger-synchronouswrite
        Force the built-in file logger to write the log synchronously.
        Useful for scenario where you don't want to lose any log (i.e. in case of crash).
        Note that this is slowing down the test execution.

    --diagnostic-output-directory
        Output directory of the diagnostic logging.
        If not specified the file will be generated inside the default 'TestResults' directory.

    --diagnostic-output-fileprefix
        Prefix for the log file name that will replace '[log]_.'

    --diagnostic-verbosity
        Define the level of the verbosity for the --diagnostic.
        The available values are 'Trace', 'Debug', 'Information', 'Warning', 'Error', and 'Critical'.

    --exit-on-process-exit
        Exit the test process if dependent process exits. PID must be provided.

    --help
        Show the command line help.

    --ignore-exit-code
        Do not report non successful exit value for specific exit codes
        (e.g. '--ignore-exit-code 8;9' ignore exit code 8 and 9 and will return 0 in these case)

    --info
        Display .NET test application information.

    --list-tests
        List available tests.

    --minimum-expected-tests
        Specifies the minimum number of tests that are expected to run.

    --results-directory
        The directory where the test results are going to be placed.
        If the specified directory doesn't exist, it's created.
        The default is TestResults in the directory that contains the test application.

    --timeout
        A global test execution timeout.
        Takes one argument as string in the format <value>[h|m|s] where 'value' is float.


Extension options:
    --coverage
        Collect the code coverage using dotnet-coverage tool

    --coverage-output
        Output file

    --coverage-output-format
        Output file format. Supported values: 'coverage', 'xml' and 'cobertura'

    --coverage-settings
        XML code coverage settings

    --filter
        Filters tests using the given expression. For more information, see the Filter option details section. For more information and examples on how to use selective unit test filtering, see https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/testing/selective-unit-tests.

    --maximum-failed-tests
        Specifies a maximum number of test failures that, when exceeded, will abort the test run.

    --no-ansi
        Disable outputting ANSI escape characters to screen.

    --no-progress
        Disable reporting progress to screen.

    --output
        Output verbosity when reporting tests.
        Valid values are 'Normal', 'Detailed'. Default is 'Normal'.

    --report-trx
        Enable generating TRX report

    --report-trx-filename
        The name of the generated TRX report

    --settings
        The path, relative or absolute, to the .runsettings file. For more information and examples on how to configure test run, see https://learn.microsoft.com/visualstudio/test/configure-unit-tests-by-using-a-dot-runsettings-file#the-runsettings-file

    --test-parameter
        Specify or override a key-value pair parameter. For more information and examples, see https://learn.microsoft.com/visualstudio/test/configure-unit-tests-by-using-a-dot-runsettings-file#testrunparameters

data.content: The property I used doesn't exist in this version. The test runner help shows `--ignore-exit-code` and `--minimum-expected-tests` options. Let me use the correct MSBuild approach:

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered in this assembly -->
    <TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>false</TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Exit code 8 = zero tests found; treat as success for conditionally-compiled assemblies -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

data.reasoningText:
Interesting. The log shows "Zero tests ran" and the exit code from `dotnet run` was 5 (because of the unknown `--nologo` flag). But the key thing is: the `dotnet test` integration is still reporting failure.

I notice from the help output there's an `--ignore-exit-code` option: `--ignore-exit-code 8` which would ignore exit code 8. But the MSBuild property should have worked too.

Let me check what's going on. The `TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests` might not be the right property name, or it might be a different version. Let me check the MSTest version and documentation.


Actually, looking at the help output more carefully, I see the testing platform has different options available. The `--ignore-exit-code` flag can suppress specific exit codes, and there's also `--minimum-expected-tests` to set a threshold. The MSBuild property approach might need to use `--ignore-exit-code 8` instead, or the property name could be different in this version of MSTest. I need to find the right MSBuild property to pass the `--ignore-exit-code 8` flag through `dotnet test`. The `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` property looks like the correct approach for passing command line options to Microsoft.Testing.Platform in MSTest 3.x. I can pass the ignore-exit-code flag directly through the command line or set it as a property in the project file. Let me try updating the csproj with the correct TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments property instead of the one I used before.

arguments.old_str:
<!-- Return exit code 0 (not 8) when no tests are discovered in this assembly -->
    <TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>false</TestingPlatformExitProcessOnMissingTests>
arguments.new_str:
<!-- Exit code 8 = zero tests found; treat as success for conditionally-compiled assemblies -->
    <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>

@@ -1,14 +1,14 @@
 <Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
   <PropertyGroup>
     <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
     <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
     <EnableMSTestRunner>true</EnableMSTestRunner>
     <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>
     <!-- Exit code 8 = zero tests found; treat as success for conditionally-compiled assemblies -->
     <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
   </PropertyGroup>
   <ItemGroup>
     <PackageReference Include="MSTest" Version="3.8.0" />
   </ItemGroup>
 </Project>

data.content:
**The fix:** Add `--ignore-exit-code 8` via the `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` MSBuild property.

**What happened:** Microsoft.Testing.Platform (the new runner behind `EnableMSTestRunner`) returns **exit code 8** when zero tests are discovered. VSTest returned 0 in this case. Since your `#if INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` compiles out all tests in some configurations, the runner flags it as an error.

**Results verified:**
- Without `INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` → 0 tests, **exit code 0** ✅
- With `INCLUDE_SLOW_TESTS` → 1 test passes, **exit code 0** ✅
